A dark log turns against the current where logs should drift downstream. The crocodile waits until someone steps into the shallows.
Spears rise from the reeds at a shallow ford marked with bone charms. The lizardfolk claim the crossing and demand payment in food or steel.
A clear coil of water rises from a ruined millrace and holds its shape against the current. It strikes at anyone disturbing the old channel.
The river surface looks smooth, but a pale sandbar shifts just below it. Reading the ripples reveals a safe line across.
A pile of driftwood shudders against a half-submerged stump. Crossing it sends sharp branches snapping upward around legs and packs.
A cold spring bubbles into the river through a bed of reeds. Its clean water soothes heat, bruises, and the sour taste of river mud.
A sealed bottle knocks against reeds beside a slow eddy. The note inside is water-stained but still asks for help from upstream.
A ferry rope has snapped and the boat is wedged against rocks downstream. Repairing the crossing before dark could save stranded travelers.
A small shrine bell rings under the river whenever clouds cover the sun. Villagers want it recovered before the next flood.
Bright watercress grows in a cold side channel, cleanest where the current is strongest. The roots cling hard to slick stones.
Brown reedmace heads stand above the marshy bank. Harvesting them before they burst provides food and useful fiber.
Wild mint scents a shaded bank where the soil stays damp. Cutting sprigs without uprooting the patch leaves it healthy.
Trout hold in a clear eddy behind a boulder. A patient cast lands them before the current carries the line into snags.
A muddy undercut bank hides whiskered catfish. Reaching them means bracing against the current without collapsing the bank.
Flat stones in the shallows hide crayfish and quick little claws. A basket placed downstream catches the ones that bolt.
Heavy gravel settles in the inside bend after a flood. Panning it patiently reveals a few bright flecks among black sand.
A cut bank exposes a shelf of smooth gray clay. It comes away cleanly if dug before the current undercuts the bank again.
Red jasper pebbles gleam beneath shallow water. Sorting them from common river stones takes a careful eye.
A jam of drift logs has built against a bend, leaving several straight trunks caught above the waterline. Freeing them shifts the whole pile.
Willows grow thick along the bank, their shoots long and flexible. Cutting the straight poles cleanly keeps the stand alive.
An abandoned dock slumps into the river, its outer planks waterlogged but its inner boards sound. Salvage must happen before the current tears it loose.
Reeds lean the wrong way along the bank as a skiff slides from cover. Hooks catch the party's boat before the pirates show themselves.
The ford runs unnaturally clear over a ring of old coins. When anyone reaches down, the river rises as a grasping column.
Croaking voices demand a toll from half-submerged stones. When refused, mud and spears fly before anyone sees all the attackers.
Knife marks on riverside posts record old ferry depths and flood seasons. Reading them shows where wagons can cross safely today.
A wall of brown water roars around the bend with broken branches at its front. Fast action gets people and packs above the bank.
A cold spring feeds the river beneath a curtain of willow roots. Its water clears dust, fever, and road aches for a little while.
A loaded barge turns slowly in an eddy with no crew aboard and dinner still warm in the galley. A wet rope trails behind it.
The ferry chain has been cut cleanly and dragged into deep water. Without it, travelers are trapped on both banks by nightfall.
A bottle trapped in a fish weir holds a coded plea from upriver. The wax seal belongs to a town that claims all is well.
A shrine bell rings from under the river whenever the moon rises. Locals say it was silent until the last pilgrim vanished.
A reed maze hides a half-sunk safehouse stocked for fugitives. Someone left blood on the door and a map to the next bend.
Bright mint grows where the bank seeps cold water. Cutting it above the root keeps the patch fresh for another harvest.
Cattails stand thick in a quiet backwater. The floury heads are useful if gathered before rain mats them down.
Young willow bark peels cleanly after a wet night. It must be taken from fallen branches, not living trunks.
Silver dace race upstream in glittering sheets. A net across the narrowest channel fills quickly if anchored well.
A heavy catfish noses through the mud at a deep bend. It pulls like a mule once hooked.
Crayfish crowd under the stones of an old ferry landing. They snap at fingers but fill a pot fast.
Agates gleam in a gravel bar after the river drops. The best stones hide just below the wet surface.
A fresh cutbank exposes a band of clean gray clay. Cutting slabs quickly matters before the river undercuts the ledge.
Rust-red gravel marks an iron pan in the shallows. Sorting useful nodules from river stone takes patience.
A cottonwood trunk has lodged against the bank after a flood. It is waterlogged but sound once cut above the mud line.
An abandoned ferry has settled into the reeds. Several planks remain tarred, straight, and worth prying free.
A square-cut mill beam has snagged below a bend, still marked with chalk numbers. Freeing it means fighting the current and old nails.
